The Math We Render

Scientific and technical articles embed equations in prose. Two rendering engines dominate: KaTeX, which leaves the original LaTeX in a semantic annotation, and MathJax, whose source lives in a script tag. A round-trippable extractor must recover the LaTeX from both without leaking the rendered spans that a non-visual consumer cannot read.

The canonical mass-energy equivalence is rendered inline by KaTeX as E=mc2E = mc^2E=mc2, a relationship Einstein published in 1905. The rendered spans carry no recoverable signal on their own; only the annotation preserves the LaTeX source.

A display equation from KaTeX shows the integral of a decaying exponential over the positive reals:

∫0∞e−x dx\int_0^\infty e^{-x}\,dx∫0∞​e−xdx

A third form, bare MathML, carries the LaTeX in a semantic annotation inside the math element itself — the convention arXiv's HTML rendering (ar5iv) and MDN use. The hidden state of a sequence model is rendered inline:

At each step the hidden state hth_{t} depends on the previous state and the current input. The MathML element characters must not leak into the markdown; only the annotation's LaTeX should survive.

A display equation in the same bare-MathML form shows a sum of two Greek letters, with rendered MathML element characters alongside the annotation:

α+β\alpha + \beta

MathJax v2 and v3 stash the LaTeX in a script tag whose MIME type marks inline versus display. The Pythagorean theorem appears inline:

The relation holds for every right triangle, regardless of the lengths of its legs. A converter that ignores the script tag strips the source entirely along with every other script on the page.

A display equation shows the closed form of the sum of the first n positive integers, contributed by MathJax in display mode:

Together these six equations exercise every engine and both display modes; an extractor that recovers them leaves downstream consumers with first-class mathematics instead of garbage text.
